---
title: "Data Analysis Advanced"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Advanced]{.level-badge .level-advanced} · **Data & Analysis** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Data & Analysis track](data-analysis.qmd){.track-back}

*Scaffold full pipelines with AI — then audit the subtle failure modes.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Use AI to scaffold a multistep modeling pipeline
- Audit AI-built pipelines for leakage, validation, and metric choice
- Know the limits of AI on tabular and time-series data

## Overview

For advanced work, AI can scaffold a full pipeline — feature prep, modeling, evaluation. The failure modes get subtler: data leakage, the wrong validation strategy, overconfident metrics. Your job is to audit each step and the assumptions behind it.

Recent work cautions that "foundation models" don't automatically win on tabular or time-series data — classic methods often match or beat them. Skepticism is part of the method.

## Practice activities

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Activity 1 · Advanced — Audit a pipeline

1. Have AI build a small modeling pipeline on **public** data.
2. Audit it for **data leakage, validation strategy, and metric choice**.
3. Fix one real flaw and document what you changed.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Activity 2 · Advanced — Compare approaches

1. Ask AI to compare a **simple baseline** against a **complex model** on your task.
2. Decide which is justified and why — complexity should earn its place.
:::

## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "A subtle, high-impact error in AI-built pipelines is…", "options": ["Data leakage", "A typo in a label", "Slow runtime", "A missing chart"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Leakage inflates performance and is easy to miss — audit for it."}, {"q": "On tabular data, foundation models…", "options": ["Don't automatically beat classic methods", "Always win", "Can't be used", "Are required"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Evidence shows classic methods often match or beat them on tabular data."}, {"q": "Reproducibility for an AI-assisted analysis means…", "options": ["Logging prompts, versions, and seeds", "Saving a screenshot", "Trusting the output", "Nothing extra"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Record prompts, tool versions, and random seeds so others can reproduce it."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

From the AI readings library (ask the facilitator for access):

- **Are Foundation Models Ready for Your Production Tabular Data?** — limits of large models on tabular tasks.
- **Is Boosting Still All You Need for Tabular Data?** — a modeling reality check.
- **Against Time-Series Foundation Models** — skepticism on forecasting hype.
- **Implementing Statistical Guardrails for Non-Deterministic Agents** — keeping AI pipelines reliable.

## Useful resources

- [tidymodels](https://www.tidymodels.org) — modeling and validation framework in R.
- [scikit-learn](https://scikit-learn.org) — machine learning in Python.
- [Quarto](https://quarto.org) — reproducible analysis documents.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Audit on **public** data first; document prompts, versions, and seeds for reproducibility. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
